title: Emotional Literacy
objective: Students will explore emotional literacy concepts including Naming Basic Emotions, Feelings Change and Differ, Triggers and Causes of Feelings and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Naming Basic Emotions? Let's find out together!
Name and recognise basic emotions — happy, sad, angry, scared, excited, and surprised — in themselves and in others by looking at facial expressions and body language
Time to try this out: Label at least five basic emotions from pictures or real situations
If you sees a friend crying at a birthday party, can they tell you what that friend might be feeling and name the emotion?
Great job! Today we learned about Naming Basic Emotions. Label at least five basic emotions from pictures or real situations Match facial expressions to emotion words in a sorting activity
Have you ever thought about Feelings Change and Differ? Let's find out together!
Understand that everyone has feelings, that feelings change throughout the day, and that the same event can make different people feel different things
Let me show you something cool. Give an example of a time their feelings changed during a single day
If you and you watch the same film and you find it funny but you finds it scary, can they understand why you both felt differently?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Feelings Change and Differ: Give an example of a time their feelings changed during a single day Explain why two children might feel differently about the same event
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Triggers and Causes of Feelings!
Understand that feelings have causes — something happens (a trigger) and that makes us feel a certain way — and begin to identify what triggers their own emotions
Let's practice together! Describe a trigger for at least three different emotions from their own experience
When you gets upset about something at school, can they explain to you what happened that made them feel that way?

Great job! Today we learned about Triggers and Causes of Feelings. Describe a trigger for at least three different emotions from their own experience Complete 'I felt ___ because ___' sentences accurately
Let's explore Expressing Feelings with Words! Have you ever wondered about this?
Express their own feelings appropriately using words rather than actions — saying 'I feel angry because...' instead of hitting, shouting, or withdrawing
Time to try this out: Use an 'I feel ___ when ___' statement during a disagreement
When you is frustrated — say a sibling takes their toy — do they use words to say how they feel rather than snatching it back or crying?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Expressing Feelings with Words: Use an 'I feel ___ when ___' statement during a disagreement Choose to tell an adult how they feel instead of acting out physically
